dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Thank you for being here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I guess, um, I don't know... maybe we could talk about this wedding my cousin invited me to? I mean, I didn’t even respond to the invitation. It just feels like a lot of stress and I can imagine all the questions people would ask. It’s just not something I want to deal with, you know? And it’s like, do they even really want me there? I don’t want to be the odd one out in the family, feeling all awkward and out of place.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like you're feeling quite overwhelmed by the idea of attending the wedding, and there’s a lot of anxiety around how you might be perceived by your family. You mentioned not wanting to feel like the odd one out and worrying about the questions people might ask. Can you share a bit more about what specifically feels stressful for you about this situation?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Yeah, I mean, it’s just the whole thing feels like a lot, you know? Like, I can already picture myself sitting there, and people would probably start asking questions about what I’m doing with my life, or why I’m still single, or whatever. And honestly, I don’t really have great answers for that, so it just feels like I'm setting myself up for failure. Plus, I can’t shake this feeling that they don’t really want me there anyway. Like, maybe they’re just being polite by inviting me. It’s just... stressful. I don’t know, it makes me feel really anxious thinking about it.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like you're feeling a lot of pressure around the expectations that might come from attending the wedding, especially regarding personal questions about your life and relationship status. I hear that this concern makes you feel anxious and possibly even unwelcome. Can you tell me more about what those questions bring up for you? What kind of feelings or thoughts do they stir?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I don’t know, it’s just like... those questions make me feel really small, you know? Like, I can imagine them looking at me with that judgmental look, and I just get this wave of embarrassment. It’s like I’m supposed to have everything figured out, but I don’t. I mean, I can’t even tell my family the truth about how I feel or what’s going on with me. I just feel like I’m going to disappoint them, and that’s a feeling I really don’t want to deal with. So it’s easier just to avoid the whole thing. Less stress that way, right?

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like those potential questions really amplify feelings of shame and embarrassment for you, almost as if you're carrying the weight of expectations that feel impossible to meet. I can hear how much you want to avoid that disappointment, both for yourself and for your family. You mentioned feeling small and judged—can you share more about what it feels like to carry that sense of pressure?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I don’t know, it just feels heavy, you know? Like, I’m supposed to be this person who has it all together, but I just feel... lost. When I think about my family looking at me with those judgmental eyes, it makes me want to shrink away, like I’m not even worthy of being there. And then there’s this nagging thought that if I don’t have a good answer, they’ll think I’m a failure or something. It’s like, I already feel that way a lot, and I just don’t want to put myself in a situation where it’s going to be thrown in my face. So yeah, avoiding it feels way easier, less chance of feeling judged or ashamed.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like the pressure to present yourself in a certain way is really weighing on you, and the thought of being judged by your family intensifies that feeling of being lost and unworthy. It must be really difficult to navigate those emotions, especially when you’re already feeling vulnerable. I wonder, what do you think it would take for you to feel more at ease with the idea of attending, or even just responding to the invitation?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I don’t really know what it would take, honestly. It just feels like too much right now. Like, even the thought of having to respond to the invitation makes my stomach churn. I guess if I could just go and not have to answer any questions, that would help. But that seems impossible, you know? I mean, who can just show up to a wedding and not talk to anyone? I just wish I could disappear into the background or something. But then I think, maybe they’d notice I wasn’t there, and then it’s like, what’s the point? I don’t know, it’s all just too overwhelming.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like the idea of responding to the invitation feels really daunting for you, and you wish you could simply blend into the background to avoid the pressure of social interactions. I can sense that there's a conflict between wanting to be there for your family and the fear of being put on the spot. What do you think it would feel like if you could express that desire to be there while also setting some boundaries for yourself?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I don’t know, I guess it would feel... weird? Like, I can’t just show up and be like, "Hey, I want to be here, but don’t ask me anything." That just feels like it would raise more eyebrows or make it awkward. I mean, I’d love to be there for my cousin, but I just can’t shake this feeling that nobody really wants me there anyway. Setting boundaries sounds nice in theory, but in reality, it just feels like I’d be inviting more scrutiny. I don’t want to make things worse; it’s just easier to avoid it all together.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like you’re feeling caught between wanting to support your cousin and the fear that expressing your needs would only draw more attention to you. I hear how difficult it is to imagine navigating that situation without feeling overwhelmed. You mentioned that setting boundaries feels like it could invite more scrutiny, which adds to your hesitance. Can you tell me more about where that feeling of being unwanted comes from? Has this been a recurring theme in your experiences with family or in other areas of your life?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I don’t know, it just feels like I've always been on the outside looking in, you know? Like, I can't really remember a time when I felt, I don’t know, genuinely wanted or included. With my family, it’s like there's this expectation to fit into a mold, and I just never have. I mean, I’ve always felt different, especially with my weight and everything. It’s like they’ve got their lives together, and then there’s me, still trying to figure things out. I guess maybe that's where this feeling of being unwanted comes from? It’s just easier to think they don’t really care if I’m there or not. I don’t know, maybe I’m just overthinking it all.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like there’s a long history of feeling like you’ve been on the outside, and that sense of not fitting into the family mold contributes to your feelings of being unwanted. I can see how that would make the idea of attending a family event feel even more daunting. You mentioned your weight and how that might play into these feelings—can you share more about how that has affected your sense of belonging or how you see yourself in relation to your family?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I don't know, it just feels like... my weight has always been this thing that sets me apart, you know? Like, I can picture family gatherings where everyone is just... normal, and then there's me. It makes me feel like I don't belong, like I'm just this extra person in the room. I mean, I can’t help but think that they see me differently, and that makes it hard to connect. It’s like I’m always worried about being judged, and it just reinforces that feeling of being unwanted. I guess I just try to keep my distance to avoid that pain. It's easier that way, but it still hurts, you know?

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like your weight has become a significant part of how you perceive your place within the family, leading to feelings of isolation and the fear of being judged. I hear that trying to keep your distance feels like a way to protect yourself from that pain, even though it still hurts. It’s understandable to want to avoid situations where you feel vulnerable. Can you tell me more about how this has shaped your relationships with family members or how you connect with them?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I guess it’s just hard to connect with them, you know? Like, I feel like I’m always on the defensive, trying to protect myself from whatever judgment might come my way. I mean, I don’t really share much about my life, and it’s like this wall I’ve built up. I don’t want to be the person who brings the family down or makes things awkward. So, I tend to just keep to myself, and that makes it feel like I’m not really part of the family at all. It’s frustrating, but at the same time, it feels like the safest option. I don’t know if that makes sense, but it’s just... complicated.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like you're navigating a really tough balance between wanting to feel connected to your family and protecting yourself from potential judgment. The wall you’ve built seems like a coping mechanism to help you avoid feeling vulnerable, but it also leaves you feeling disconnected and frustrated. I can sense the complexity of wanting to belong while also feeling like you need to shield yourself. How do you think this wall affects your relationship with your family, and what do you wish could be different in those connections?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I don’t know, it just feels like it keeps me at a distance, you know? Like, I can’t really engage with them or share what’s going on in my life because I’m always worried about what they’ll think. It’s frustrating because I want to feel connected, but then I build up this wall to protect myself, and it just ends up making things worse. I guess if I could just be more open, it might help, but then I think about all the judgment and just... I don’t know, it feels safer to keep it all in. I wish things could be different, but I just don’t know how to break that barrier.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like you're feeling really stuck between the desire for connection and the instinct to protect yourself from judgment. I can hear how much you wish things could be different, and that desire to be more open is there, but the fear of judgment makes it feel so challenging. It must be exhausting to carry that tension. What do you think might help you explore that barrier a little more? What would it feel like to take small steps toward being more open, even if it's just in your thoughts right now?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I don’t know, it just feels like a lot to think about, you know? Like, being open sounds nice in theory, but it’s like, what if I try and it just leads to more judgment? I mean, I can imagine just saying something and then everyone’s looking at me like I’m crazy or something. I guess maybe I could start with just thinking about what I want to say, but even that feels overwhelming. I just... I don’t want to set myself up for disappointment. It’s easier to just keep things to myself and avoid all that pressure. I don’t know if I’m ready to take any steps right now.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like the idea of being open feels really daunting and brings up a lot of fear about potential judgment, which makes it hard to even consider taking steps in that direction. I hear you when you say that keeping things to yourself feels like the safer option right now. It’s completely understandable to feel that way, especially given everything you’ve shared about your experiences. What do you think it would feel like to simply acknowledge that fear without needing to act on it just yet? How might that shift your perspective?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I guess acknowledging the fear sounds... reasonable? But it’s hard to even think about it without feeling that knot in my stomach. Like, I can see why I’d want to avoid it, but just sitting with that fear feels heavy too. I mean, it’s like, I know it’s there, but the idea of facing it or even just admitting it feels like a big deal. I don’t know if I want to go there right now. It’s just... I don’t know, overwhelming. It’s like I’m stuck in this loop of trying to protect myself, but I also want to feel connected. I just wish there was an easier way to navigate all of this.
